requirements.english: IELTS preferred (TOEFL iBT or Duolingo accepted), complete by Dec 31; waived with an undergraduate degree of 3+ years from an English-language university in an English-speaking country.
requirements.admission_model_note: A departmental graduate admissions committee admits students to the program, not to individual advisors; students choose a thesis advisor during the first year or two after exploring courses and seminars.

funding.note: All admitted students are offered support (fellowship/TA/RA covering tuition and stipend); there is no separate financial aid application. Guaranteed number of years not stated on the admissions page.

requirements.contact_note: Admissions questions go to the Math Graduate Office (gradofc@math.mit.edu); pre-application faculty contact is not part of the process.

faculty.name: Jörn Dunkel
faculty.title: MathWorks Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dunkel leads MIT's physical applied mathematics effort on living and active systems, developing continuum models and data-driven methods for biology. Recent work spans bacterial biofilm growth, active turbulence and chiral/odd fluids, curvature-guided pattern formation in development, and learning interpretable dynamical models directly from microscopy and single-cell data. His group combines nonlinear PDEs, topology, and statistical inference to explain self-organization in biological matter, collaborates extensively with experimentalists, and supervises PhD students on projects ranging from morphogenesis to network and knot dynamics.

faculty.name: Steven G. Johnson
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Johnson works on high-performance scientific computing and mathematical methods for wave phenomena, and is known for co-creating FFTW and foundational computational photonics tools (MPB, Meep). Recent research centers on large-scale inverse design and topology optimization of photonic and electromagnetic devices, fast methods for integral equations, special-function computation, and rigorous physical bounds on light-matter interactions. He bridges numerical analysis and physics, is heavily involved in the Julia and open-source ecosystems, and advises students on problems combining optimization, discretization theory, and device design.

faculty.name: Philippe Rigollet
faculty.title: Cecil and Ida Green Distinguished Professor, Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Rigollet chairs MIT's Applied Mathematics Committee and works at the intersection of statistics, optimization, and machine learning theory. His recent research develops mathematical foundations for modern ML: statistical optimal transport, Wasserstein gradient flows, sampling viewed as optimization over probability measures, and a widely noted program analyzing transformers and self-attention as interacting particle systems that explains token clustering in deep architectures. Earlier contributions span high-dimensional statistics and aggregation. His students train in probability, analysis of learning dynamics, and applied statistics.

faculty.name: Laurent Demanet
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Professor of Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences
faculty.summary: Demanet directs MIT's Imaging and Computing Group, focusing on inverse problems for wave propagation with applications in seismic and geophysical imaging. His research combines applied harmonic analysis, fast algorithms, and increasingly machine learning: low-frequency extrapolation for full-waveform inversion, learned regularization and priors for imaging, and stability analysis of inverse scattering. Holding a joint appointment in Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences, he supervises students on problems spanning numerical analysis, signal processing, and ML-assisted inversion for the Earth sciences and beyond.

faculty.name: John Urschel
faculty.title: Class of 1956 Career Development Assistant Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Urschel works in matrix analysis, numerical linear algebra, and spectral graph theory. Recent papers address eigenvalue perturbation bounds, graph Laplacians and their use in partitioning and graph drawing, determinantal point processes, and theoretical foundations of widely used algorithms such as Lanczos-based eigensolvers and low-rank approximation schemes. He is also known for expository and historical writing on linear algebra and for sustained mathematics outreach and mentoring. His research gives PhD students problems blending rigorous analysis with algorithmic questions from scientific computing and data science.

faculty.name: Martin Bazant
faculty.title: E. G. Roos (1944) Professor of Chemical Engineering; Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Bazant develops mathematical theory and models for transport, reactions, and interfaces in electrochemical systems. His group formulated theories of diffuse-charge dynamics, induced-charge electro-osmosis, and nonequilibrium thermodynamics of ion intercalation that reshaped understanding of batteries and electrokinetics. Recent work spans lithium-ion battery modeling, electrodeposition and dendrite control, water desalination and purification, and physics-based machine learning for electrochemical data. Bridging applied mathematics and chemical engineering, he applies these tools to energy storage, sustainability, and medical physics problems.

faculty.name: John Bush
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Bush studies interfacial fluid dynamics, combining experiments, theory, and mathematics. He is a leader in hydrodynamic quantum analogs, showing that millimetric droplets walking on a vibrating fluid bath can mimic quantum behaviors such as quantized orbits, tunneling, and statistical interference through pilot-wave dynamics. His group also explores surface-tension-driven flows, biological locomotion at small scales, and the fluid mechanics of insects and plants. Recent work refines the theoretical foundations of walking-droplet systems and their connections to de Broglie's pilot-wave picture.

faculty.name: Alan Edelman
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Edelman works in numerical linear algebra, random matrix theory, and high-performance scientific computing. He is a co-creator of the Julia programming language, designed for fast, expressive technical computing, and leads the Julia Lab at MIT. His research connects the statistics of random matrix eigenvalues to applications across physics, statistics, and data science, and advances parallel and GPU computing, automatic differentiation, and algorithms for large-scale linear algebra. Recent efforts integrate machine learning with scientific computing through differentiable programming in Julia.

faculty.name: Anette Hosoi
faculty.title: Neil and Jane Pappalardo Professor of Mechanical Engineering; Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Hosoi works at the intersection of fluid dynamics, biomechanics, and bio-inspired design. Her group studies the mechanics of soft, deformable systems and how organisms move through and manipulate fluids, translating biological strategies into engineered devices and robots. She has analyzed snail locomotion, digging shellfish, and the physics of thin films and complex fluids. More recently she applies applied mathematics and data science to sports analytics, quantifying performance and strategy. She co-founded the MIT Sports Lab connecting researchers with teams and industry.

faculty.name: Bonnie Berger
faculty.title: Simons Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Berger designs algorithms that extract biological insight from massive, rapidly growing datasets. Her group pioneered compressive genomics, enabling analyses that scale sublinearly with exploding sequence databases, along with methods for network integration, structural bioinformatics, and single-cell analysis. She also develops privacy-preserving techniques for sharing genomic and medical data. Recent work applies machine learning to protein structure and function, drug discovery, and immunology, combining rigorous computer science with biology to address problems in genomics, medicine, and public health.

faculty.name: Gigliola Staffilani
faculty.title: Abby Rockefeller Mauzé Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Staffilani studies dispersive nonlinear partial differential equations using tools from harmonic analysis, probability, and analytic number theory. Her work establishes well-posedness, conservation, and long-time behavior for equations such as the nonlinear Schrodinger and Korteweg-de Vries equations, including landmark results on global well-posedness and energy transfer between frequencies. Recent research develops the mathematical theory of wave turbulence, deriving effective kinetic equations describing how energy cascades across scales in nonlinear dispersive systems, and applies probabilistic methods to random data and invariant measures.

faculty.name: Semyon Dyatlov
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dyatlov works in microlocal analysis, scattering theory, and quantum chaos, studying how classical dynamics governs solutions of wave and Schrodinger equations at high energy. With collaborators he introduced the fractal uncertainty principle, using it to prove spectral gaps for chaotic scattering and lower bounds on eigenfunction mass on hyperbolic surfaces. His research also treats Pollicott-Ruelle resonances of chaotic flows, decay of waves, and mathematical aspects of general relativity, linking dynamical systems, semiclassical analysis, and spectral theory.

faculty.name: Larry Guth
faculty.title: Claude E. Shannon (1940) Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Guth works in harmonic analysis, combinatorial and metric geometry, and their deep interconnections. He is known for introducing polynomial partitioning, a method that resolved longstanding incidence problems and drove major progress on restriction, Kakeya, and decoupling estimates in Fourier analysis. His work connects the geometry of tubes and lines, extremal combinatorics, and analytic inequalities, and he has contributed to systolic geometry and sharp forms of the restriction conjecture. Recent efforts push toward the Kakeya conjecture and refined Fourier-analytic estimates.

faculty.name: Tobias Holck Colding
faculty.title: Cecil and Ida Green Distinguished Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Colding is a geometric analyst working on minimal surfaces, mean curvature flow, and the analysis of geometric partial differential equations. With William Minicozzi he developed a comprehensive structure theory for embedded minimal surfaces and, later, foundational results on singularities and generic behavior of mean curvature flow. His work also spans harmonic functions on manifolds, Ricci curvature, and the geometry of spaces with lower curvature bounds. Recent research studies uniqueness of tangent flows, singularity models, and quantitative geometric estimates.

faculty.name: William Minicozzi
faculty.title: Singer Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Minicozzi is a geometric analyst renowned for his long collaboration with Tobias Colding on minimal surfaces and mean curvature flow. Together they proved deep results classifying embedded minimal surfaces and analyzing singularities of curvature flows, including work on generic mean curvature flow and uniqueness of tangent cones. His research combines partial differential equations, differential geometry, and analysis to understand how surfaces evolve and degenerate. Recent work examines singularity formation, arrival-time equations, and the quantitative behavior of geometric flows.

faculty.name: Bjorn Poonen
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor in Science; Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Poonen works in number theory and arithmetic geometry, focusing on rational and integral points on algebraic varieties. He has developed methods for determining solutions to Diophantine equations, advanced the study of the Brauer-Manin obstruction, and produced influential results on undecidability related to Hilbert's tenth problem over rings. His research spans elliptic curves, the arithmetic of curves and their Jacobians, and heuristics for ranks and Selmer groups. Recent work refines Chabauty-type methods and statistical models for arithmetic objects.

faculty.name: Wei Zhang
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Zhang works in number theory, automorphic forms, and arithmetic geometry, studying deep relationships between L-functions and arithmetic. He has made major contributions to the Gan-Gross-Prasad conjectures, the arithmetic fundamental lemma, and generalizations of the Gross-Zagier formula relating special cycles to derivatives of L-functions. His research connects Shimura varieties, Rankin-Selberg periods, and trace-formula techniques. Recent work develops arithmetic relative trace formulas and higher analogues linking intersection numbers of algebraic cycles to central derivatives of automorphic L-functions.

faculty.name: Pavel Etingof
faculty.title: David W. Skinner Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Etingof works in representation theory, quantum groups, and mathematical physics. His research spans tensor categories, rational Cherednik algebras, symplectic reflection algebras, and the interplay between noncommutative algebra and integrable systems. He has contributed foundational results on quantization, Hecke algebras, and the structure and classification of fusion and tensor categories. His work connects algebra, geometry, and physics, and he actively mentors young researchers as chief research adviser of MIT-PRIMES. Recent efforts explore representation theory in positive characteristic and new categorical structures.

faculty.name: Alexander Postnikov
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Postnikov works in algebraic combinatorics and its links to geometry, representation theory, and physics. He is known for foundational work on total positivity and the positive Grassmannian, introducing plabic graphs and combinatorial parametrizations now central to cluster algebras and scattering amplitudes. His research also encompasses generalized permutohedra, Schubert calculus, symmetric functions, and the combinatorics of polytopes and root systems. Recent work continues to develop the combinatorics of positive geometries, networks, and their surprising connections to modern mathematical physics.

faculty.name: Scott Sheffield
faculty.title: Leighton Family Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sheffield is a probabilist studying random geometric structures arising in statistical physics and quantum field theory. He has been central to developing the theory of the Gaussian free field, Schramm-Loewner evolution, and Liouville quantum gravity, establishing rigorous connections between random surfaces, conformal field theory, and scaling limits of discrete models. His work links percolation, random growth, and imaginary geometry. Recent research constructs and analyzes Liouville quantum gravity as a random metric space and explores its deep relationship with random planar maps.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT 90+ (or IELTS, recommended overall band 7.0); waived only with a completed degree from an institution where the medium of instruction is English.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted to the Division of Applied Mathematics as a whole, not to a particular professor; advisor selection happens after arrival during the first year of study.

funding.note: At least five years of guaranteed funding, including stipend, tuition, health services fee, and health insurance.

requirements.contact_note: The Division notes you may email a professor directly with specific research questions, but admission is to the division rather than to individual advisors.

faculty.name: George Em Karniadakis
faculty.title: The Charles Pitts Robinson and John Palmer Barstow Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Karniadakis leads the CRUNCH group, a driving force in scientific machine learning: he co-introduced physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and the DeepONet operator-learning framework. Recent work develops neural operators for multiphysics and multiscale systems, uncertainty quantification for scientific ML, fractional and stochastic PDEs, and applications ranging from blood flow and biomedicine to materials and fluid mechanics. His group co-develops widely used open-source SciML tools and collaborates with national labs and industry. Students typically combine deep learning, numerical analysis, and concrete scientific applications.

faculty.name: Björn Sandstede
faculty.title: Alumni-Alumnae University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sandstede studies nonlinear dynamics and pattern formation, combining rigorous dynamical-systems theory with computation and, increasingly, data-driven methods. Recent projects include spiral waves and their instabilities, localized patterns and snaking bifurcations, stability of nonlinear waves, agent-based and network models in biology, and machine-learning approaches to model identification and parameter inference. He is deeply involved in graduate training and undergraduate research mentoring at Brown, and his group's work spans mathematical analysis, scientific computing, and applications from cell polarization to ecology and cardiac dynamics.

faculty.name: Chi-Wang Shu
faculty.title: Theodore B. Stowell University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Shu is a leading figure in high-order numerical methods for PDEs, particularly weighted essentially non-oscillatory (WENO) schemes and discontinuous Galerkin methods, which he continues to refine for stability, positivity, and bound preservation. Recent work addresses entropy-stable and structure-preserving discretizations for hyperbolic conservation laws, convection-dominated problems, and applications in computational fluid dynamics, astrophysics, and kinetic and traffic models. His methods underpin many production simulation codes worldwide, and his students receive deep training in numerical analysis and scientific computing.

faculty.name: Kavita Ramanan
faculty.title: Roland George Dwight Richardson University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Ramanan works in probability theory and stochastic processes: large deviations, interacting particle systems, mean-field and local weak convergence limits, and stochastic networks with reflecting dynamics. Recent research develops rigorous frameworks for sparse-graph limits of interacting systems, hydrodynamic limits for load-balancing networks, and high-dimensional probability with connections to statistical learning and data science applications. She leads significant mentoring and outreach initiatives and advises PhD students on problems combining deep probabilistic theory with models drawn from engineering, physics, and operations research.

faculty.name: Brendan Keith
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Keith develops numerical methods for PDEs and computational optimization, with recent emphasis on adaptive finite element methods, PDE-constrained optimization under uncertainty, and the proximal Galerkin method for variational inequalities and pointwise-constrained problems. Other active directions include machine-learning-augmented discretizations and scalable solvers developed with collaborators at national laboratories. As an early-career faculty member holding a named assistant professorship, he is actively building his group, and his projects blend functional analysis, optimization, and high-performance scientific computing.

faculty.name: Mark Ainsworth
faculty.title: Francis Wayland Professor of Applied Mathematics; Director of Graduate Studies
faculty.summary: Ainsworth is the Francis Wayland Professor and Director of Graduate Studies in Applied Mathematics. His research centers on the numerical approximation of partial differential equations, including finite element methods, a posteriori error estimation, adaptivity, and high-performance scientific computing. He works on wave propagation, spectral and high-order methods, and hierarchical multiscale modeling, holding a joint appointment at Oak Ridge National Laboratory. A SIAM Fellow, he bridges rigorous numerical analysis with large-scale computation for challenging engineering and physical applications.

faculty.name: Johnny Guzmán
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Division Associate Chair
faculty.summary: Guzmán is a Professor and Division Associate Chair whose research focuses on the numerical analysis of partial differential equations. He develops and analyzes finite element methods, including mixed, hybridizable, and discontinuous Galerkin schemes, as well as structure-preserving and divergence-free discretizations. His work addresses elliptic problems on nonsmooth domains, elasticity, and incompressible fluid mechanics, combining rigorous error analysis with practical scientific computing. He is known for constructing stable, high-accuracy finite element spaces that respect the underlying physics of the equations.

faculty.name: Jerome Darbon
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Darbon is a Professor of Applied Mathematics working at the interface of optimization, partial differential equations, and scientific computing. His research connects efficient algorithms with Hamilton-Jacobi equations arising in optimal control, differential games, and variational and Bayesian estimation. With Stanley Osher he developed methods for overcoming the curse of dimensionality in certain high-dimensional Hamilton-Jacobi problems, an award-winning contribution. He also studies fast optimization for imaging and links between control, neural networks, and real-time computation.

faculty.name: Paul Dupuis
faculty.title: IBM Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dupuis is the IBM Professor of Applied Mathematics and a leading probabilist. His research applies probability to the control of deterministic and stochastic systems, emphasizing large deviation theory, stochastic control, and numerical methods for simulating rare events. He co-developed the weak-convergence approach to large deviations and pioneered importance-sampling schemes grounded in stochastic control and viscosity solutions. His work spans queueing and stochastic networks, Monte Carlo methods, and accelerated sampling, bridging rigorous probability with practical computation.

faculty.name: Hongjie Dong
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dong is a Professor of Applied Mathematics specializing in the analysis of partial differential equations. His research addresses regularity theory for nonlinear elliptic and parabolic equations, the Navier-Stokes and quasi-geostrophic equations, and problems arising in composite materials. He also studies probabilistic approaches to PDEs and rates of convergence for finite-difference approximations of elliptic and parabolic Bellman equations from stochastic control. Combining harmonic analysis with PDE techniques, his work delivers sharp estimates for equations with rough or discontinuous coefficients.

faculty.name: Yan Guo
faculty.title: L. Herbert Ballou University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Guo is the L. Herbert Ballou University Professor of Applied Mathematics and an expert in partial differential equations from kinetic theory and mathematical physics. His research studies the Boltzmann equation for dilute gases, Landau and Vlasov-Maxwell systems in plasma physics, and Vlasov-Poisson models for stellar dynamics, often focusing on nonlinear stability of steady states. He also analyzes classical fluid models governed by Euler and Navier-Stokes equations and their derivation from kinetic descriptions, developing techniques for boundary effects and hydrodynamic limits.

faculty.name: Govind Menon
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Division Chair
faculty.summary: Menon is the Division Chair and a Professor of Applied Mathematics whose work sits at the interface of dynamical systems, probability, and mathematical physics. He studies the kinetics of phase transitions and coarsening, integrable systems, random matrix theory, and statistical theories of turbulence, including Burgers turbulence. Recent interests include the formation and propagation of disorder in physical models and numerical algorithms, self-assembly, and mathematical questions arising in deep learning. His research blends rigorous analysis with modeling and computation.

faculty.name: Carina Curto
faculty.title: Pablo J. Salame Goldman Sachs Professor of Computational Neuroscience and Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Curto is the Pablo J. Salame Goldman Sachs Professor of Computational Neuroscience and Professor of Applied Mathematics, affiliated with the Carney Institute for Brain Science. Trained in mathematical physics, she now works at the intersection of mathematics and theoretical neuroscience. Her research develops the theory and analysis of neural networks and neural codes using tools from applied algebra, topology, and geometry. She studies how recurrent circuits support learning, memory, and sequence generation in cortical and hippocampal systems, linking network structure to computation.

faculty.name: Mara Freilich
faculty.title: Thomas J. and Alice M. Tisch Assistant Professor of Earth, Environmental, and Planetary Sciences and Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Freilich is the Tisch Assistant Professor of Earth, Environmental, and Planetary Sciences and Applied Mathematics, and is affiliated with the Data Science Institute. She uses mathematical modeling, observations, and data analysis to study ocean and ecological dynamics in the climate system. Her research spans fluid dynamics of ocean fronts and eddies, submesoscale processes, phytoplankton and microbial ecology, and reaction-advection-diffusion and stochastic models. She connects physical oceanography with biological productivity and carbon cycling, and pursues community-engaged science for climate justice.

faculty.name: Matthew Harrison
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Harrison is an Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics working in statistics, machine learning, and computational neuroscience. His research develops methods for conditional inference and multiple hypothesis testing and for detecting patterns in multi-neuronal spiking data. Affiliated with the BrainGate project, he designs statistical learning and prediction algorithms for online neural control of brain-computer interfaces. His work, published in venues such as Nature Neuroscience and the Annals of Statistics, blends rigorous statistical theory with exploratory data analysis of complex neural recordings.

faculty.name: Stuart Geman
faculty.title: James Manning Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Geman is the James Manning Professor of Applied Mathematics and a member of Brown's Pattern Theory group. He has made influential contributions to computer vision, probability, statistics, machine learning, and computational neuroscience. With his brother Donald Geman he introduced the Gibbs sampler and gave the first convergence proof for simulated annealing, foundational tools in Bayesian computation. His research explores compositionality, reusability, and hierarchy as principles for efficient learning in biological and artificial systems, developing probabilistic models of vision and natural scenes.

faculty.name: Oanh Nguyen
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Nguyen is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics working in probability. Her research studies random structures and their phase transitions, including random polynomials, random graphs and matrices, Markov chains, and optimal transport. A central theme is universality, understanding when limiting behavior is independent of fine model details. She has analyzed survival and extinction of epidemics and the contact process on random graphs, and the distribution of roots of random polynomials, publishing in journals such as the Annals of Probability.

faculty.name: Youngtak Sohn
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sohn is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics whose research lies in probability theory and its interactions with statistical physics, theoretical computer science, and statistics. He studies mean-field spin glasses, random constraint satisfaction problems, and the analysis of stochastic networks, together with mathematical foundations of deep learning. His work on multi-species and spherical spin glass models establishes variational formulas for the limiting free energy and characterizes replica symmetry breaking, connecting statistical mechanics with high-dimensional inference and optimization.

faculty.name: Yair Shenfeld
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Shenfeld is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics, affiliated with the Data Science Institute, working in high-dimensional probability and its links to analysis, geometry, and mathematical physics. His research spans optimal transport, stochastic analysis, convex geometry, functional inequalities, and renormalization-group methods. He studies extremal problems in convex geometry, displacement convexity, and the mathematical underpinnings of generative modeling. Trained at Princeton and MIT, he develops geometric and probabilistic tools that clarify concentration, transportation, and inequalities in high dimensions.

faculty.name: Hui Wang
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics; Director of Undergraduate Studies
faculty.summary: Wang is an Associate Professor and Director of Undergraduate Studies in Applied Mathematics, working on probabilistic methods and their applications. His research covers stochastic optimization, mathematical finance, Monte Carlo simulation, large deviations, and stochastic networks. Earlier work produced explicit solutions for path-dependent options under double-exponential jump-diffusion models and convex-duality methods for portfolio optimization in incomplete markets. More recently he has developed fast simulation and game-theoretic importance-sampling schemes for estimating rare-event probabilities, and he co-authored a textbook on Monte Carlo methods in finance.

faculty.name: J. Nathan Kutz
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Kutz develops data-driven methods for complex systems, co-inventing sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy), dynamic mode decomposition variants, and deep-learning approaches to coordinate discovery and reduced-order modeling. Recent work targets interpretable machine learning for dynamics, sparse sensing and optimal sensor placement, multiscale and spatiotemporal systems, and applications from fluids and optics to neuroscience. He co-directs the NSF AI Institute in Dynamic Systems and has co-authored standard textbooks on data-driven science, placing his group squarely at the interface of applied mathematics, ML, and dynamical systems.

faculty.name: Steven L. Brunton
faculty.title: Adjunct Professor of Applied Mathematics; Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Brunton, an adjunct professor in Applied Mathematics with his primary appointment in Mechanical Engineering, works on machine learning for fluid dynamics and control. Recent research includes SINDy and its many extensions, physics-informed and interpretable machine learning, reduced-order models of turbulence, machine-learning control of unsteady flows, and digital twins for engineering systems. He co-directs the NSF AI Institute in Dynamic Systems, co-authored the influential 'Data-Driven Science and Engineering' textbook, and mentors students who are often co-supervised across engineering and applied mathematics.

faculty.name: Bamdad Hosseini
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Hosseini works on the mathematical foundations of probabilistic scientific computing. His recent research develops Bayesian inverse problem theory for heavy-tailed and non-Gaussian priors, kernel and Gaussian-process methods for solving nonlinear PDEs, theoretical analysis of operator learning, and sampling and conditional simulation via transport and triangular maps. He connects approximation theory, probability, and numerical analysis, with collaborations in imaging and data assimilation. As an assistant professor he is actively developing his group within UW's strong data-driven applied mathematics environment.

faculty.name: Bernard Deconinck
faculty.title: Boeing Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Deconinck studies nonlinear wave phenomena: integrable systems, water waves, and the stability of periodic and solitary traveling waves. Recent work analyzes high-frequency instabilities of Stokes waves, spectral stability computations for nonlinear wave equations, the unified transform (Fokas) method for boundary-value and interface problems, and computational tools for Riemann surfaces and integrable PDEs. As Boeing Professor of Applied Mathematics he has led curriculum and textbook development in the department, and he advises students on projects mixing rigorous analysis, perturbation methods, and numerical computation.

faculty.name: Eric T. Shea-Brown
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Shea-Brown works in mathematical and computational neuroscience, analyzing how network connectivity shapes collective dynamics and computation in the brain. Recent research covers low-dimensional structure in large recurrent networks, links between connectome statistics and neural correlations, the dimensionality of neural activity, and comparisons between trained artificial networks and biological circuits, often with collaborators at the Allen Institute for Brain Science. His group uses random matrix theory, dynamical systems, and machine learning, and PhD students typically combine theory with analysis of large-scale neural recordings.

faculty.name: Leslie Greengard
faculty.title: Silver Professor of Mathematics and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Greengard co-invented the fast multipole method, one of the most influential algorithms in scientific computing, and continues to develop fast, high-accuracy solvers based on integral-equation formulations of PDEs. Recent work includes nonuniform fast Fourier transforms (the FINUFFT library), solvers for Stokes and electromagnetic problems in complex geometry, and computational methods for cryo-electron microscopy and biomedical imaging. He has also led the Flatiron Institute's Center for Computational Mathematics, giving students exposure to both Courant and Flatiron research environments.

faculty.name: Eric Vanden-Eijnden
faculty.title: Paulette Goddard Chair in Mathematics
faculty.summary: Vanden-Eijnden develops mathematical and computational methods for stochastic systems, rare events, and multiscale dynamics; his transition path theory and string methods are standard tools in chemistry and materials science. Recent work focuses on the theory of machine learning: mean-field analyses of neural networks, score-based generative models and stochastic interpolants for sampling and free-energy computation, and ML-assisted importance sampling of rare events such as extreme weather. His group blends applied probability, statistical mechanics, and deep learning, spanning Courant's applied math and ML communities.

faculty.name: Georg Stadler
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Stadler works on PDE-constrained optimization, large-scale Bayesian inverse problems, and optimization under uncertainty, with flagship applications in geophysics: ice-sheet dynamics, earthquake rupture, and mantle flow. Recent research develops scalable methods for inference with expensive forward models, stochastic optimization for PDE-governed systems, and high-order and mixed discretizations, frequently deployed on leading supercomputers. He is deeply involved in Courant's scientific computing curriculum and advises students who combine numerical analysis, optimization, and high-performance computing on science-driven problems.

faculty.name: Jonathan Weare
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Weare designs and analyzes Monte Carlo methods for high-dimensional sampling and rare-event problems. Recent work includes ensemble and affine-invariant samplers, umbrella sampling and stratification schemes with rigorous error analysis, fast randomized iteration methods for quantum chemistry, and statistical estimation of rare climate events such as heat waves using hierarchies of models. His projects connect applied probability with concrete applications in molecular simulation, materials science, and climate, often in collaboration with domain scientists, making his group a strong fit for students in stochastic numerics.

faculty.name: Benjamin Peherstorfer
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Mathematics (Courant Institute)
faculty.summary: Peherstorfer works on scientific machine learning and model reduction at Courant. Recent contributions include online-adaptive and nonlinear model reduction for transport-dominated problems, operator inference for learning low-dimensional dynamics from data, multifidelity Monte Carlo and optimization methods for uncertainty quantification, and neural-network-based schemes such as random feature and Neural Galerkin solvers for high-dimensional PDEs. He has received major early-career recognition in computational science and advises students combining numerical analysis, data-driven modeling, and applications from aerospace to plasma physics.

programs.program_name: MSc in Mathematical Modelling and Scientific Computing (MMSC)

requirements.english: English proficiency at Oxford's higher level (approximately IELTS 7.5 with 7.0 minimum per component, or TOEFL iBT 110); standard Oxford exemptions apply — verify current bands.
requirements.admission_model_note: Taught 12-month MSc admitted centrally via the Mathematical Institute; dissertation supervisors are matched during the year for a roughly four-month project, sometimes proposed and co-supervised by industrial partners.

funding.note: Most students self-fund; applying by the January deadline gives automatic consideration for Oxford scholarships (e.g., Clarendon) — over 1,100 full or partial graduate scholarships are offered university-wide.

requirements.contact_note: As a taught course there is no supervisor matching before admission; questions go to the course administrator or Course Director (Dr Kathryn Gillow).

faculty.name: Patrick Farrell
faculty.title: Professor, Mathematical Institute; Tutorial Fellow, Oriel College
faculty.summary: Farrell develops numerical methods for nonlinear PDEs: structure-preserving and high-order finite element discretizations, fast preconditioners and multigrid solvers, and bifurcation analysis of PDEs via his deflation techniques for computing multiple solution branches. Recent applications include liquid crystals, non-Newtonian and magnetohydrodynamic flows, and semismooth methods for variational inequalities, with much of the work shipped in the Firedrake and PETSc software ecosystems. He teaches core numerical analysis material relevant to the MMSC and supervises MSc dissertations and DPhil students on discretizations, solvers, and computational modelling.

faculty.name: Coralia Cartis
faculty.title: Professor of Numerical Optimization, Mathematical Institute; Tutorial Fellow, Balliol College
faculty.summary: Cartis works on continuous optimization: worst-case complexity and regularization methods for nonconvex problems, derivative-free and stochastic optimization, and random-subspace and sketching techniques for large-scale computation. Recent research addresses dimensionality reduction for optimization, tensor and higher-order methods, global optimization of functions with low effective dimension, and applications in machine learning and climate/data-assimilation inverse problems. She is affiliated with the Alan Turing Institute, teaches optimization material taken by MMSC students, and supervises dissertations at the interface of optimization, numerical analysis, and data science.

faculty.name: Yuji Nakatsukasa
faculty.title: Professor, Mathematical Institute (Numerical Analysis Group)
faculty.summary: Nakatsukasa works in numerical linear algebra and approximation theory. He co-developed the AAA algorithm for rational approximation, now a standard computational tool, and his recent research centers on randomized numerical linear algebra: fast, stable algorithms for least-squares, low-rank approximation, eigenvalue and singular-value problems, and randomized sketching with rigorous error analysis. Further interests include spectral methods and connections to random matrix theory, machine learning, and data science. He teaches numerical analysis within the MSc and supervises projects on fast algorithms for large-scale scientific computation.

faculty.name: S. Jon Chapman
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and its Applications; Head of Department, Mathematical Institute
faculty.summary: Chapman is a leading applied analyst and modeller specializing in asymptotic methods — notably exponential asymptotics — applied to PDEs arising in industry, physics, and biology. His recent work spans free-boundary problems, high-frequency wave scattering, superconductivity and vortex dynamics, dislocations in elastic crystals, mathematical medicine and drug delivery, and discrete-to-continuum limits. Based at the heart of Oxford's industrial applied mathematics community (OCIAM) and currently Head of Department, he supervises numerous MSc case studies and dissertations, exemplifying the modelling stream of the MMSC course.

faculty.name: Philip K. Maini
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematical Biology; Director, Wolfson Centre for Mathematical Biology
faculty.summary: Maini directs the Wolfson Centre and is a leading figure in mathematical biology. His group develops and analyses continuum and discrete models of biological pattern formation, morphogenesis and wound healing, drawing heavily on reaction-diffusion theory. Recent work spans collective cell migration in development and cancer, tumour growth and angiogenesis, epithelial dynamics, and the interplay between mechanics and chemical signalling in tissues. He collaborates closely with experimental biologists to test model predictions and emphasises multiscale approaches linking cell-level behaviour to tissue-scale outcomes.

faculty.name: Ruth Baker
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Baker works at the interface of applied mathematics and quantitative biology, developing stochastic and multiscale models of cell and developmental processes together with the statistical machinery to fit them to data. Recent research focuses on efficient simulation and coarse-graining of agent-based models, Bayesian parameter inference and identifiability, and model selection for biological systems. Applications include collective cell migration, tissue growth, and neural crest development. A recurring theme is bridging detailed individual-based descriptions and tractable continuum equations while rigorously quantifying uncertainty.

faculty.name: Radek Erban
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Erban develops multiscale mathematical and computational methods for stochastic modelling in biology and chemistry. His work connects molecular-level Brownian and reaction-diffusion dynamics to mesoscopic and macroscopic descriptions, designing algorithms that couple different levels of detail across space and time. Recent themes include stochastic reaction-diffusion simulation, self-organisation and pattern formation, DNA/molecular dynamics, and models of collective animal behaviour. A central aim is rigorous, computationally efficient coarse-graining that preserves the essential stochastic features of biochemical and biophysical systems.

faculty.name: Helen Byrne
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematical Biology
faculty.summary: Byrne is a leader in mathematical oncology and tissue modelling. Her research uses continuum multiphase and agent-based models to study tumour growth, angiogenesis, drug delivery and the tumour microenvironment, increasingly combined with topological data analysis to quantify spatial structure in biological images. Recent work links mechanics, nutrient transport and cell signalling in tumours and organoids, and integrates mathematical models with quantitative experimental and imaging data. She emphasises translational applications aimed at informing cancer treatment strategies and understanding vascular and tissue-level dynamics.

programs.program_name: Program in Applied and Computational Mathematics (PACM) PhD

requirements.english: TOEFL or IELTS required for applicants whose native language is not English; exemption if prior instruction was in English (Princeton generally expects TOEFL iBT ~100+).

First-year students take a preliminary exam and work with first-year advisers, then choose a dissertation adviser (program or associated faculty) in consultation with the Director of Graduate Studies.

funding.note: All admitted PhD students receive full tuition plus a stipend for the normal period of enrollment through fellowships and assistantships.

requirements.contact_note: Committee-based admission; contacting faculty in advance is not required, though understanding faculty research strengthens the statement.

faculty.name: Amit Singer
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics; Director of PACM
faculty.summary: Singer develops mathematical and computational methods for data science and structural biology, and currently directs PACM. He is best known for algorithms in single-particle cryo-electron microscopy, using representation theory, spectral graph methods, optimization and applied harmonic analysis to reconstruct 3D molecular structures and handle heterogeneity. Recent work addresses ab initio modelling, orientation estimation, denoising and covariance estimation for cryo-EM, as well as broader problems in dimensionality reduction, synchronization over groups and manifold learning. His research blends rigorous mathematics with high-performance numerical methods on noisy, high-dimensional data.

faculty.name: Peter Constantin
faculty.title: William R. Kenan Jr. Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Constantin is a leading analyst of nonlinear PDEs arising in fluid dynamics and mathematical physics. His research concerns the Euler and Navier-Stokes equations, active scalars such as the surface quasi-geostrophic equation, turbulence, and mixing, with recent work on regularity, blow-up criteria, incompressible flows, and models coupling fluids to biology and chemistry (e.g. chemotaxis and buoyancy-driven transport). He combines rigorous functional-analytic estimates with physical insight to understand long-time behaviour, singularity formation and statistical properties of complex fluid systems.

faculty.name: Ramon van Handel
faculty.title: Professor of Operations Research and Financial Engineering / PACM
faculty.summary: Van Handel works in probability theory, particularly high-dimensional and geometric probability. His research develops sharp tools for understanding the behaviour of random structures, including non-asymptotic random matrix theory, concentration of measure, chaining and comparison inequalities for Gaussian and empirical processes, and connections between probability and convex geometry. Recent work establishes optimal bounds for the norms and spectra of random matrices and explores functional inequalities and their applications. His results provide foundational estimates used across statistics, data science and theoretical machine learning.

faculty.name: Howard Stone
faculty.title: Donald R. Dixon '69 and Elizabeth W. Dixon Professor of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering
faculty.summary: Stone is a fluid dynamicist whose work spans microfluidics, complex and interfacial flows, and biophysical transport. His group combines asymptotic analysis, scaling arguments and experiments to study low-Reynolds-number and multiphase flows, droplet and bubble dynamics, flows in porous and confined geometries, and the mechanics of bacteria, biofilms and soft materials. Recent research addresses fluid-structure interactions, transport in networks, and the physics of biological and environmental flows. His applied-mathematics-oriented approach makes fundamental flow phenomena tractable and connects theory to engineering and biological applications.

faculty.name: Maria Chudnovsky
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Chudnovsky is a combinatorialist known for deep results in structural graph theory. She co-proved the Strong Perfect Graph Theorem and works on the structure of graph classes defined by forbidden induced subgraphs, colouring problems, and the Erdos-Hajnal conjecture. Recent research develops structural decompositions and polynomial-time algorithms for problems on graphs with excluded patterns, and explores connections between induced subgraph structure, chromatic number and stable sets. Her work combines intricate combinatorial arguments with algorithmic consequences, contributing foundational understanding of when hard graph problems become tractable.

faculty.name: Andrea Bertozzi
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics; Director of Applied Mathematics; Betsy Wood Knapp Chair for Innovation and Creativity
faculty.summary: Bertozzi is a leader in applied mathematics, directing UCLA's applied program. Her research spans nonlinear partial differential equations, fluid dynamics of thin films, and data science. Recent work develops graph-based and PDE methods for machine learning and image classification, models of aggregation and swarming, particle-laden and thin-film flows, and mathematical approaches to crime modelling and social dynamics. A hallmark is connecting rigorous PDE and variational analysis to real applications, including semi-supervised learning on large graphs, hyperspectral image analysis, and cooperative control of robotic swarms.

faculty.name: Terence Tao
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics (James and Carol Collins Chair)
faculty.summary: Tao is one of the most influential pure mathematicians, working across harmonic analysis, analytic and additive number theory, combinatorics, random matrix theory and nonlinear PDE. His work includes results on prime patterns, the structure of arithmetic sets, universality for random matrices, and regularity and blow-up questions for dispersive and fluid equations. Recent research spans problems in analytic number theory, combinatorial analysis, and, increasingly, the use of formal proof assistants and AI-assisted tools in mathematics. He is known for building bridges between seemingly distant areas and for prolific collaboration.

faculty.name: Marcus Roper
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Computational Medicine
faculty.summary: Roper applies mathematical modelling and fluid dynamics to understand how cells and organisms solve physical challenges. His group studies transport in fungal mycelial networks, blood flow and microcirculation, spore dispersal, and the physics of microbial life, combining asymptotic analysis, PDE modelling and close collaboration with experimentalists. Recent work examines optimality and self-organisation in biological transport networks, red-blood-cell partitioning in microvasculature, and quantitative modelling of fungal biology. His research links detailed biological data to tractable mathematical models revealing design principles of living systems.

faculty.name: Mason Porter
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Porter works in applied mathematics on networks, complex systems and nonlinear dynamics. His research develops methods for multilayer and temporal networks, community detection, dynamical processes on networks, and topological data analysis, with applications ranging from physical and biological systems to social science. Recent work studies opinion and information spreading on social networks, models of contagion, and structure in data from the physical, biological and information sciences. He emphasises mathematically principled tools for uncovering structure and dynamics in complex, interconnected systems and connecting them to empirical data.

faculty.name: Deanna Needell
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics; Dunn Family Endowed Chair
faculty.summary: Needell works in computational and applied mathematics at the interface of signal processing, optimization and machine learning. Her research develops and analyses efficient algorithms for compressed sensing, sparse recovery, randomized numerical linear algebra, stochastic iterative methods, and nonnegative matrix factorization, with rigorous theoretical guarantees. Recent work addresses large-scale and online optimization, topic modelling and interpretable machine learning, and applications to medical imaging and social-good problems. She combines probabilistic and functional-analytic tools with practical algorithm design for high-dimensional data.

faculty.name: Rowan Killip
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Killip works in analysis, spectral theory, and nonlinear dispersive partial differential equations. With frequent collaborator Monica Visan he has advanced the well-posedness and long-time behavior theory for equations such as the nonlinear Schrodinger and Korteweg-de Vries equations, including sharp results at critical regularity and on the role of conserved quantities. He also studies spectral and inverse-spectral theory of Schrodinger and Jacobi operators, orthogonal polynomials, and random operators. He was invited to speak at the 2026 International Congress of Mathematicians.

faculty.name: Monica Vișan
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Visan studies nonlinear dispersive partial differential equations, especially the nonlinear Schrodinger and Korteweg-de Vries equations. She is known for resolving critical well-posedness and scattering problems, treating equations on domains with boundaries, and building invariant measures and low-regularity theory using tools from harmonic analysis and, more recently, completely integrable structure. Her method of commuting flows has produced sharp global results. Named a 2026 ICM invited speaker and AWM-AMS Emmy Noether Lecturer, she frequently collaborates with Rowan Killip.

faculty.name: Chandrashekhar Khare
faculty.title: Professor and Chair of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Khare works in algebraic number theory, focusing on Galois representations and their links to modular and automorphic forms. With Jean-Pierre Wintenberger he proved Serre's modularity conjecture, work recognized by the 2011 Cole Prize in Number Theory. His research addresses modularity lifting, deformation theory of Galois representations, and reciprocity between arithmetic and automorphy. He currently serves as chair of the UCLA Department of Mathematics. Recent work continues to explore modularity, class field theory analogues, and the arithmetic of elliptic curves.

faculty.name: Ko Honda
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Honda works in contact and symplectic geometry, low-dimensional topology, and their connections to dynamics and mathematical physics. He developed convex surface theory and classification results for tight contact structures on three-manifolds, and contributed to the foundations of contact homology, Heegaard Floer theory, and bordered invariants. His research links pseudo-holomorphic curve techniques with the topology of three- and four-manifolds. Recent work advances higher-dimensional contact topology and functorial, sheaf-theoretic approaches to Floer-theoretic invariants of manifolds with boundary.

faculty.name: Sucharit Sarkar
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sarkar works in low-dimensional topology, focusing on knot and link invariants and the homological algebra behind them. With collaborators he constructed a stable homotopy refinement of Khovanov homology, giving a space-level invariant of links, and developed combinatorial and computational approaches to Heegaard Floer homology. His research probes relationships between gauge-theoretic, symplectic, and combinatorial invariants of knots, three-manifolds, and four-manifolds. Recent work studies functoriality, spectral refinements, and structural properties of Khovanov and Floer homology theories.

faculty.name: Burt Totaro
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Totaro works in algebraic geometry, often bringing tools from topology and homotopy theory to bear on cycles, cohomology, and moduli. He is known for foundational contributions to the Chow ring of classifying spaces, the study of algebraic cycles and their torsion, and questions of rationality and stable rationality of algebraic varieties. His research also spans Hodge theory, the geometry of Fano varieties, and cohomology of algebraic groups. Recent work examines torsion algebraic cycles and obstructions to rationality.

faculty.name: Alexander Merkurjev
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Merkurjev works in algebra, especially algebraic K-theory, quadratic forms, and cohomological invariants of algebraic groups. His celebrated theorem with Suslin relating the Brauer group to K-theory transformed the field and underpinned later proofs of the Milnor and Bloch-Kato conjectures. He studies central simple algebras, essential dimension, motivic cohomology, and Galois cohomology of linear algebraic groups. A recipient of the AMS Cole Prize in Algebra, he continues research on essential dimension and cohomological invariants.

faculty.name: Igor Pak
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Pak works in combinatorics and its interactions with probability, algebra, and computational complexity. His research spans enumerative and bijective combinatorics, partition theory, combinatorial inequalities, and the complexity of counting problems, including which combinatorial quantities admit efficient formulas. He has studied random generation, tilings, integer points in polytopes, and the algorithmic hardness underlying log-concavity and unimodality phenomena. Recent work probes combinatorial inequalities from an algebraic and probabilistic viewpoint and their computational-complexity aspects, connecting enumeration to questions in theoretical computer science.

faculty.name: Itay Neeman
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Neeman is a set theorist working on inner model theory, determinacy, forcing, and large cardinals. He has made major contributions connecting the strength of determinacy axioms to inner models with Woodin cardinals, and developed techniques for iteration trees and long games. His research also treats forcing constructions, the tree property, and combinatorial set theory. A Simons Fellow and recipient of the Hausdorff Medal, he is affiliated with the UCLA Logic Center and mentors students in mathematical logic.

faculty.name: Inwon Kim
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Kim studies partial differential equations, with emphasis on free boundary problems and nonlinear evolution equations arising in physics and biology. Her work analyzes existence, regularity, and asymptotic behavior for models of fluid flow, phase transitions, crowd motion, and tumor growth, including connections between porous-medium and Hele-Shaw type limits. She uses viscosity solution methods, optimal transport, and gradient-flow techniques. Recent research treats aggregation-diffusion equations, congestion models, and the emergence of interfaces in nonlinear diffusion.

faculty.name: Wilfrid Gangbo
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Gangbo works in the calculus of variations, nonlinear partial differential equations, and optimal transportation. He is known for foundational results on the existence and structure of optimal transport maps for general cost functions, and for applying transport ideas to fluid mechanics, kinetic theory, and mean field games. His research develops Hamilton-Jacobi equations on spaces of measures and Wasserstein geometry. A recipient of the 2026 AMS Award for Distinguished Public Service, he continues work on high-dimensional mean field problems.

faculty.name: Stanley Osher
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics; Director of Special Projects, IPAM
faculty.summary: Osher is a leader in numerical analysis and scientific computing whose methods are used across physics, imaging, and data science. He co-invented level set methods for evolving interfaces, ENO and WENO shock-capturing schemes for hyperbolic conservation laws, and total-variation-based models for image denoising and reconstruction. His work also spans fast optimization for sparse and compressed sensing problems and Hamilton-Jacobi equations. Director of Special Projects at IPAM, he continues developing algorithms linking PDEs, optimization, and machine learning.

faculty.name: Guido Montúfar
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Mathematics and Statistics & Data Science
faculty.summary: Montufar develops the mathematical foundations of machine learning, leading the Mathematical Machine Learning Group at UCLA and the Max Planck Institute for Mathematics in the Sciences. His research analyzes the expressive power of deep neural networks, the geometry of their function spaces and loss landscapes, and the dynamics of training and optimization. He draws on information geometry, algebraic statistics, and optimal transport to understand generalization and representation. A Sloan Fellow, his recent work studies gradient dynamics, implicit bias, and reinforcement learning theory.

faculty.name: Marek Biskup
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Biskup works in probability theory and mathematical statistical mechanics. His research studies the large-scale behavior of disordered and interacting systems, including phase transitions, random walks in random environments, and models of magnetism and percolation. He has made notable contributions to the extremal theory of the two-dimensional discrete Gaussian free field and to random conductance models. He leads the UCLA probability group. Recent work analyzes extreme values, local times, and scaling limits of log-correlated random fields.

faculty.name: Luminita Vese
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Vese works in applied mathematics, developing variational and partial-differential-equation methods for image processing and computer vision. She is known for active-contour and region-based models for image segmentation, including widely used level-set formulations building on the Mumford-Shah functional, as well as methods for image denoising, decomposition into cartoon and texture, and restoration. Her research applies these tools to medical and scientific imaging. Recent work advances variational models, numerical schemes, and multiscale representations for image analysis and reconstruction.

faculty.name: Eric Cytrynbaum
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Cytrynbaum works in mathematical and computational cell biology, studying how cells organise themselves in space and time. His research uses ODE/PDE models, dynamical systems and simulation to understand the cytoskeleton, mitotic spindle positioning, bacterial cell division and pattern formation of intracellular structures. Recent work addresses self-organisation of protein systems, microtubule dynamics, and geometry-sensing mechanisms in cells, often in collaboration with experimental biophysicists. He emphasises mechanistic models that connect molecular-scale interactions to emergent cellular-scale spatial organisation and testable biological predictions.

faculty.name: Daniel Coombs
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics; Head, Department of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Coombs applies mathematics to immunology, infectious disease and cell biophysics. His research models the dynamics of immune receptors and T-cell recognition, within-host and population-level dynamics of viral infections such as HIV, and the statistical analysis of single-particle tracking data to infer molecular motion on cell surfaces. Recent work spans epidemiological modelling, quantitative analysis of membrane receptor organisation, and stochastic models linking molecular events to cellular decisions. He combines PDE, stochastic-process and statistical methods with close collaboration between mathematics, immunology and physics.

faculty.name: Michael J. Ward
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Ward is an applied mathematician specialising in singular perturbation theory and the analysis of localized patterns in PDEs. His research develops matched asymptotic and hybrid analytical-numerical methods to study spot, spike and stripe solutions of reaction-diffusion systems, narrow-escape and first-passage problems, and dynamics of concentrated structures relevant to chemistry and biology. Recent work analyses pattern formation, stability and dynamics of localized states in models such as Gierer-Meinhardt and Gray-Scott, and diffusion in domains with small traps. His work provides sharp asymptotic understanding of strongly nonlinear pattern-forming systems.

faculty.name: James J. Feng
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and of Chemical and Biological Engineering
faculty.summary: Feng works in the modelling and computation of complex fluids and biological flows. His research develops continuum and computational models for viscoelastic and multiphase fluids, moving interfaces, and the mechanics of cells and tissues, combining PDE modelling with numerical simulation. Recent work addresses cell migration and morphogenesis, biofilm and mucus dynamics, droplet and interface behaviour, and fluid-structure interactions in physiological systems. He bridges applied mathematics and chemical/biological engineering, using quantitative models to illuminate the physics of soft biological and industrial materials.

faculty.name: Leah Edelstein-Keshet
faculty.title: Professor Emerita of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Edelstein-Keshet is a pioneer of mathematical biology and author of a classic textbook in the field; she is now Professor Emerita but remains research-active and was awarded the 2025 CRM-Fields-PIMS Prize. Her work models cell polarization, motility and shape change, the reaction-diffusion dynamics of Rho GTPase signalling, and collective movement in cell and animal groups. She develops PDE and ODE models tightly linked to experiments, revealing mechanisms of pattern formation and coordinated motion. As emerita she may have limited capacity to take on new PhD students.

faculty.name: Peter Scholze
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics (Hausdorff Chair)
faculty.summary: Scholze is a Fields Medalist and one of the leading figures in arithmetic geometry, based in Bonn and affiliated with the Max Planck Institute for Mathematics. His work created the theory of perfectoid spaces and reshaped p-adic geometry and the p-adic Langlands program, with tools such as prismatic cohomology and diamonds. More recently, together with collaborators, he has developed condensed mathematics, a framework unifying algebra and topology, and pursued its analytic and geometric consequences. His research connects number theory, representation theory and algebraic geometry at a foundational level.

faculty.name: Stefan Schwede
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Schwede is a leading algebraic topologist working in stable and equivariant homotopy theory. His research develops the foundations and structural theory of spectra, symmetric and orthogonal spectra, and global equivariant homotopy theory, which treats compatible actions of all compact Lie groups simultaneously. Recent work includes systematic accounts of global homotopy theory and applications to equivariant stable homotopy, using categorical and model-theoretic frameworks. His research provides organising principles and computational tools that underpin much of modern homotopy theory and its connections to algebra and geometry.

faculty.name: Herbert Koch
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Koch is an analyst specialising in nonlinear partial differential equations and harmonic analysis. His research studies well-posedness, regularity and long-time behaviour for dispersive and parabolic equations, including Schrödinger, KdV and wave-type equations, often at low regularity and in connection with integrable structure. Recent work addresses conserved energies and sharp well-posedness for dispersive models, quasilinear problems, and interactions between harmonic analysis and PDE. He develops refined analytic techniques for controlling nonlinear interactions and understanding the fine structure of solutions to fundamental evolution equations.

faculty.name: Karl-Theodor Sturm
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sturm works at the interface of probability, analysis and geometry. He is a founder of the synthetic theory of Ricci curvature bounds for metric measure spaces via optimal transport, and studies heat flow, Dirichlet forms and functional inequalities in singular geometric settings. Recent research addresses metric measure geometry, gradient flows, distances between geometric spaces, and stochastic analysis on spaces with curvature bounds, including random geometry and geometric aspects of optimal transport. His work links probabilistic tools, geometric analysis and the theory of nonsmooth spaces.

faculty.name: James J. (Jamie) Bock
faculty.title: Marvin L. Goldberger Professor of Physics; JPL Senior Research Scientist
faculty.summary: Experimental cosmology focusing on CMB polarization measurements to detect inflationary gravitational waves. Leads BICEP/Keck Array and SPIDER experiments.

faculty.name: Katherine L. (Katie) Bouman
faculty.title: Professor of Computing and Mathematical Sciences, Electrical Engineering and Astronomy; Rosenberg Scholar
faculty.summary: Computational imaging for astronomy. Led imaging of the M87 black hole shadow with the Event Horizon Telescope. Machine learning and computer vision for astrophysics.

faculty.name: Yanbei Chen
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Theoretical gravitational-wave physics and astrophysics. Black hole spectroscopy, tests of general relativity, and quantum noise in gravitational-wave detectors.

faculty.name: Katherine R. de Kleer
faculty.title: Professor of Planetary Science and Astronomy; Hufstedler Family Scholar
faculty.summary: Planetary science with focus on solar system bodies. Io's volcanic activity, planetary surface composition, and planetary atmospheres.

faculty.name: S. George Djorgovski
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy and Data Science; Director, Center for Data Driven Discovery
faculty.summary: Computational astrophysics and data science. Globular clusters, galaxy evolution, gamma-ray bursts, and the application of AI/ML to astronomical discovery.

faculty.name: Kareem J. El-Badry
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Astronomy
faculty.summary: Stellar astrophysics. Binary star populations and evolution, dormant black holes and neutron stars revealed through Gaia astrometry.

faculty.name: James (Jim) Fuller
faculty.title: Professor of Theoretical Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Theoretical astrophysics of stars. Asteroseismology, stellar interiors, tidal interactions in binary systems, and compact object formation.

faculty.name: Sunil Golwala
faculty.title: Professor of Physics; Deputy Executive Officer for Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Experimental cosmology and astrophysics. Direct dark matter searches, CMB measurements, and superconducting detector development for submillimeter astronomy.

faculty.name: Gregg W. Hallinan
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy; Director, Owens Valley Radio Observatory
faculty.summary: Radio astronomy and time-domain astrophysics. Exoplanet magnetospheres, stellar flares, and radio transients. Director of Owens Valley Radio Observatory.

faculty.name: Fiona A. Harrison
faculty.title: Harold A. Rosen Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: High-energy astrophysics. PI of NuSTAR, the first focusing hard X-ray telescope in space. Studies of black holes, neutron stars, and multimessenger astrophysics.

faculty.name: Lynne Hillenbrand
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy
faculty.summary: Observational studies of star formation and young stellar objects. Properties of protostars, circumstellar disks, and stellar populations in star-forming regions.

faculty.name: Philip F. Hopkins
faculty.title: Ira S. Bowen Professor of Theoretical Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Theoretical astrophysics of galaxy formation and evolution. Large-scale cosmological simulations (FIRE), interstellar medium physics, and black hole-galaxy co-evolution.

faculty.name: Andrew W. Howard
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy; Executive Officer for Astronomy
faculty.summary: Exoplanet detection and characterization. Radial velocity surveys for exoplanets around nearby stars. Planet population statistics and super-Earth compositions.

faculty.name: Mansi M. Kasliwal
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy; Director, Palomar Observatory
faculty.summary: Time-domain astrophysics. Optical and infrared transients, gravitational wave electromagnetic counterparts, Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF). Director of Palomar Observatory.

faculty.name: Shrinivas R. (Shri) Kulkarni
faculty.title: George Ellery Hale Professor of Astronomy and Planetary Science
faculty.summary: Pioneer of time-domain astronomy. Gamma-ray bursts, optical transients, neutron stars, and fast radio bursts. Led the Palomar Transient Factory and ZTF.

faculty.name: Christopher Martin
faculty.title: Edward C. Stone Professor of Physics; Director, Caltech Optical Observatories
faculty.summary: UV/optical astronomy and instrumentation. Galaxy evolution, the circumgalactic and intergalactic medium. Director of Caltech Optical Observatories.

faculty.name: Dimitri Mawet
faculty.title: David Morrisroe Professor of Astronomy; JPL Senior Research Scientist
faculty.summary: Exoplanet direct imaging and high-contrast instrumentation. Coronagraphy, adaptive optics, and characterization of exoplanet atmospheres with Keck and JWST.

faculty.name: Elias R. Most
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Theoretical Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Theoretical astrophysics of compact objects. Neutron star mergers as multimessenger sources, general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamics, and equation of state of dense matter.

faculty.name: E. Sterl Phinney
faculty.title: Professor of Theoretical Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Theoretical astrophysics of compact objects. Neutron stars, black holes, gravitational-wave sources, and accretion physics. Founding theorist of LISA gravitational-wave science.

faculty.name: Vikram Ravi
faculty.title: Professor of Astronomy
faculty.summary: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) and radio transient astronomy. Localization and host galaxy studies of FRBs as cosmological probes. Leads DSA-110 FRB survey.

faculty.name: Charles (Chuck) Steidel
faculty.title: Lee A. DuBridge Professor of Astronomy
faculty.summary: Observational cosmology of galaxy formation. Pioneered the Lyman-break technique for finding high-redshift galaxies. Keck Cosmic Web Imager science.

faculty.name: Alan J. Weinstein
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Experimental gravitational-wave physics. LIGO data analysis, detection of compact binary mergers, and multimessenger follow-up of gravitational-wave events.

faculty.name: Jonas Zmuidzinas
faculty.title: Merle Kingsley Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Development of superconducting detectors and instrumentation for submillimeter/Terahertz astronomy. Microwave Kinetic Inductance Detectors (MKIDs) for astrophysics.
